title: Applying PVA sealer and varnish to air-dry clay
subtitle: How to seal a finished air-dry clay piece for durability and light water resistance
excerpt: PVA and acrylic varnish are the standard finishes for air-dry clay: they consolidate the surface, reduce moisture absorption, and protect painted pieces from chipping. Correct preparation of the clay surface and correct layering of the sealer produce a finish that lasts.
sourceNotes: Sealing technique for air-dry clay derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried but unfired. The correct stage for sealing: sealing before bone-dry traps moisture inside the clay body.

content.text: Sealing air-dry clay is the last step before a piece goes into use. PVA glue diluted 1:1 with water brushes on as a first coat sealer that soaks into the surface and consolidates loose particles. Acrylic varnish over the top gives a cleaner, harder finish in matte, satin, or gloss. The piece must be fully
before any sealer is applied; sealing while damp traps moisture and causes the surface to flake.

Sand the bone-dry clay with 220-grit sandpaper for a smooth surface, or leave unsanded for a raw texture. Wipe off all dust with a soft dry cloth; any clay dust under the sealer will show as small bumps. Check for cracks; press fine clay dust or a little PVA into hairline cracks before sealing.

Mix one part PVA with one part water in a small jar. Brush the diluted PVA over the entire piece, including the base, in smooth strokes. Work quickly; PVA dries to a tacky state fast. Allow to dry fully (20-30 minutes) before applying the second coat. The piece may look slightly uneven or milky while wet; it clears as it dries.

Once the PVA wash is fully dry, apply a coat of acrylic varnish in the chosen finish. Matte reads closest to natural clay; satin is more versatile; gloss is the most water-resistant. Apply in long, even strokes without over-brushing. Let dry for the time specified on the tin before handling.

Seal before painting if a smooth undercoat is wanted (the PVA wash reduces the clay's absorbency and makes acrylic paint go further). Seal again after painting with at least one coat of varnish to protect the acrylic from scuffing.
What it looks like when it is right

The sealed surface is uniform in sheen, with no milky patches or brush-stroke ridges. Running a wet finger over the sealed surface should leave no clay residue. A sealed piece set in a shallow dish of water for one minute should show no softening of the clay surface on removal; though extended water exposure will still penetrate unsealed edges.
Sealing before the clay is fully dry. The moisture trapped underneath lifts the sealer from the surface in patches over the following days.
Over-brushing the varnish. Acrylic varnish starts to drag and leave streaks if worked too long after application. Brush on, leave; do not go back over areas that are already beginning to set.

items.symptom: Sealer peels or flakes after a few days
items.cause: Clay was not fully bone-dry before sealing
items.fix: Strip the sealer (gently with fine sandpaper), let the piece dry completely for another day or two, then re-seal

items.symptom: Varnish drips on a vertical surface
items.fix: Use thinner coats and rotate the piece so vertical faces become horizontal during drying
